food_name: Teh (Singapore kopitiam tea with sweetened condensed milk, hot)
region_of_origin: Singapore — brewed from Ceylon black tea in the kopitiam tradition; Teh is the tea counterpart to Kopi, prepared by pouring strong Ceylon tea concentrate over sweetened condensed milk in the same cloth-sock brewing method.
food_subgroup: Hot sweetened milk tea — strong, tannin-rich Ceylon black tea brewed through a cloth sock, poured over a base of sweetened condensed milk; standard kopitiam preparation.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has tea beverage as a category (FOODON_03306128). No specific entry exists for Singapore Teh (kopitiam condensed milk tea); closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea — used as closest_parent for Singapore kopitiam Ceylon black tea with sweetened condensed milk (hot); no specific FoodEx2 entry exists for Teh.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for Singapore Teh; A0KZJ (tea) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Singapore kopitiam Teh; not mapped.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 1 cup (220 ml / 220 g), as served at a Singapore kopitiam.

nutrient_source.title: Teh (condensed milk tea) — Health Promotion Board Singapore, Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match to HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Teh (condensed milk tea). Values represent the standard kopitiam preparation with sweetened condensed milk per 100 g. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index Database — condensed milk tea (analogous: sweetened condensed milk tea, GI ~80–88)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore Teh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 83 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened condensed milk tea entries in the database (GI range ~80–88), which share the same primary glycaemic driver — sweetened condensed milk (~30–40 g per cup). This is consistent with the GI profile of Singapore Kopi (same condensed milk base). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = (83 × 19.8) / 100 ≈ 16.4, rounded to 16.3 as reported by HPB.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 35 mg per 100 g; caffeine ~50–75 mg per cup
hypertension.guidance: Teh has low sodium (35 mg/100 g; ~77 mg per 220 g serving), making it a low sodium-concern beverage; however, caffeine (~50–75 mg per cup from Ceylon tea) remains a consideration for caffeine-sensitive hypertensive patients, though Teh contains approximately 50% less caffeine than Kopi (~100–150 mg), making it preferable for this group.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 83 (high), glycaemic load 16.3 per serving, sugar ~18.7 g per cup from sweetened condensed milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teh carries a high GI (~83) and a glycaemic load of 16.3 per 220 g serving, virtually identical to Kopi, because both beverages use the same 30–40 g of sweetened condensed milk as their primary sugar source; patients who switch from Kopi to Teh believing it is a healthier option achieve no glycaemic benefit — the condensed milk must be reduced or replaced (e.g., Teh Siu Dai or Teh-C Siu Dai with evaporated milk and less sugar) to meaningfully lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.9 g per 100 g (~2.0 g per serving); cholesterol 5 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is modest at 0.9 g/100 g (~2.0 g per 220 g serving) from the condensed milk component; while this is not a high saturated fat load per cup, daily multiple-cup consumption in the kopitiam context can accumulate meaningfully, and patients with cardiovascular disease should be counselled to limit frequency or switch to lower-fat milk alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh (奶茶（炼奶）, 'milk tea with condensed milk') is the classic hot tea of Singapore's kopitiam (coffee shop) culture — the direct tea counterpart to Kopi. The word 'Teh' is derived from the Hokkien pronunciation of 'tea' (茶, chá). In kopitiam vocabulary, 'Teh' without any modifier always means Ceylon black tea prepared with sweetened condensed milk, served hot. The tea is brewed from a robust, dark-roasted Ceylon tea dust blend through a flannel cloth sock filter, producing a strong, tannic concentrate. This concentrate is poured over a base of sweetened condensed milk already placed in the cup, and the drink is often 'pulled' — poured back and forth between two vessels at height — to aerate, froth, and thoroughly incorporate the milk. The result is a thick, sweet, deep amber-red tea with characteristic tannic depth, milky richness, and a smooth, slightly caramelised sweetness from the condensed milk. Teh is less caffeinated than Kopi (Ceylon tea provides approximately 50–75 mg caffeine per cup versus Robusta coffee's 100–150 mg) and contains theaflavins — the distinctive polyphenols of fully oxidised black tea associated with anti-inflammatory properties. CLINICALLY CRITICAL: Despite widespread patient belief that Teh is a healthier alternative to Kopi (often citing the tea association), the sugar and glycaemic profile is essentially identical — both use the same 30–40 g of sweetened condensed milk per cup, yielding GI ~83 and GL ~16.3 per serving. Switching from Kopi to Teh achieves no glycaemic improvement. For diabetes management, the condensed milk must be addressed: options include Teh Siu Dai (less sweet), Teh-C (evaporated milk instead of condensed milk), or Teh-O (no milk). Per 100 g: energy 50 kcal, protein 1.2 g, fat 1.4 g, carbohydrate 9.0 g, sugar 8.5 g, sodium 35 mg. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo.
meal_context: kopitiam drink pairing with kaya toast

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from an exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on sweetened condensed milk tea entries with the same glycaemic driver. All mandatory multilingual fields are populated. Confidence is high.
notes: Teh is the traditional milk tea of Singapore and Malaysia, central to kopitiam culture. It is made using a strong, dark-roasted Ceylon tea dust blend brewed in a flannel filter sock. The standard Teh is prepared by adding sweetened condensed milk to the cup first, then pouring the hot tea concentrate over it; the drink is often pulled between two containers to create a frothy top. Monounsaturated fat (-1), polyunsaturated fat (-1), and water (-1) are reported as -1 because HPB Foodo does not disaggregate fat fractions or report water content for this entry; -1 denotes not reported by source, not zero. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace in condensed milk tea at this preparation scale). Dietary fibre is 0.0 (confirmed: no fibre-contributing ingredients). The GI of 83 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Singapore Teh exists in the University of Sydney database as of the accessed date.
